title: Developer beats nine others for $15m block of Coogee beach units
text:
An original 1920s block of beachfront units at Coogee sold for $15 million before a crowd of 100 at auction on Saturday.

The weathered Art Deco building at 9 & 11 Baden Street was made up of two blocks of units with three flats in each, meaning six apartments in total.

Guided at $9.5 million, ten parties registered for the investment opportunity and six made offers. Buyers were a mix of developers, landbankers and families setting up their children.

Bidding opened at $9 million and went up in $100,000 and $50,000 rises, soaring slowly above its $11 million reserve until it sold under the hammer for $15 million.

title: Shock failure that killed 45 in one month
text:
The aged care home at the centre of a deadly Covid outbreak in July 2020 has admitted it failed to ensure all staff were properly trained.

St Basil’s Homes for the Aged in Victoria was charged by the workplace watchdog WorkSafe after 45 residents died from Covid-related complications within a month of the outbreak.

In the County Court of Victoria on Monday, the Melbourne aged care provider pleaded guilty to one count of failing to provide a safe working environment to staff between March 13 and July 12, 2020.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

According to the particulars of the charge, St Basil’s failed to provide appropriate training to all staff at the facility on the appropriate use of PPE and mitigation measures after national guidelines for residential aged care were published on March 13.

St Basil’s continues to operate as a residential aged care home. Picture: NewsWire / David Crosling

The Fawkner facility was placed into lockdown. Picture: NewsWire / Andrew Henshaw

At the start of the outbreak, St Basil’s had 92 residents and 102 staff. The not-for-profit organised five training sessions at the Fawkner facility with external medical providers for some staff between March and June, the court was told.

However, five staff members did not attend any of these sessions and not all staff working at the facility understood the appropriate measures, the court was told.

The first confirmed case of Covid came on July 9 and, by July 15, dozens of cases were identified when all staff and residents were tested.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Addressing the court, barrister Conor O’Bryan, for St Basil’s, said the company had suffered significant public backlash since the deaths but had worked to digitise records and brought on a new training manager.

Capacity at the Fawkner homes have since dropped from 150 residents to 77.

The commonwealth government took control of the facility on July 22. Picture: NewsWire / Ian Currie

Families of residents were initially barred from visiting. Picture: NewsWire / David Crosling

The facility, the court was told, had been plunged into significant financial distress after the outbreak but continued to operate after the Greek Orthodox Church, which owned the land, waived rent for several years.

St Basil’s posted a profit in excess of $1m in the financial year to July 2024 and a net loss of $847,000 in the financial year to July 2025.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

Judge Trevor Wright indicated that he was planning to impose a financial penalty for the breach when he sentences St Basil’s on March 11.

The judge said while the aged care provider had suffered over the past five years, it did not argue it would be unable to pay a fine.

The deaths of patients at the facility are the subject of an ongoing coronial inquest.

title: Entire outback Aussie towns set to be cut off for months after record rainfall: 'Need to get out'
text:
Outback towns are facing weeks of isolation after record rainfall. Incredible aerial photos show the Queensland border town of Birdsville surrounded by floodwaters.

Crews from Diamantina Shire Council have been working through the night to keep the town safe.

Mayor Francis Murray spoke to Yahoo News from Birdsville, where the water was flowing over the seven-metre-high Diamantina Bridge, which is a critical access point into town.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

While "massive amounts" of water have fallen there, he has plans to fly over to Bedourie later today because it is “more of a concern”.

“We’re going to be isolated by road for quite some time by the looks of things,” he predicted.

Flooding is a semi-regular occurrence in the region, but this year the resulting isolation is expected to be prolonged. The waters are important for wildlife and farming, but can make daily life a challenge.

Murray suspects the mental health impact of being cut off will be felt most by those who are new to the outback.

“In a month, people are going to be going, I need to get out,” he said.

Aviation company planning food drops for isolated outback towns

Shops have been well-stocked, but Birdsville Aviation is planning to provide food and medical drops in a week.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

The company’s co-owner, Talia Ellis, told Yahoo News that isolation events like this always create “an air of uncertainty” in the towns, particularly because it’s unknown how long they’ll be cut off from the rest of Australia.

“It means different things to different people,” she said.

“Some people are excited about seeing the waters rise and how the countryside will look after they eventually recede.

“But the isolation can be a bit daunting for those who need to get out of town for medical appointments.”

The road into Birdsville is underwater, isolating the town from the rest of Australia. Source: Birdsville Aviation

In tiny Bedourie, which has a population of just 150 people, the flooding has primarily been caused by localised rainfall.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

It sits higher than the surrounding desert on a dune and is protected by levees.

An estimated 200mm of rain has fallen in the region since the weather event began last week, and around 500mm has fallen further north in the Georgina River catchment, which will eventually flow south, prolonging its isolation.

The water levels are already on par with those experienced in 2011, and locals are predicting they could rival the floods of 1974, when Bedourie was cut off for around four months.

The rainfall around Birdsville has been heavy, causing widespread flooding. Source: Birdsville Aviation

Tourism boom predicted after floodwaters recede

Along with chartering planes, Talia and her husband also run the local pub in Birdsville.

With access to the town closed, business is likely to struggle over the coming weeks. They're used to short periods of isolation caused by summer rain, but this year will likely be longer.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

She’s hopeful the roads will dry up sometime in April, and then the tourist trade will quickly resume.

And when the floodwaters recede, the surrounding landscape will undergo a rapid change.

Campers, travellers, and nature-lovers will head out to outback locations like Kati Thanda–Lake Eyre as they spring to life, attracting thousands of waterbirds.

“It is exciting for us. We get a bit of short-term pain at this point in time, but as a season it results in long-term gain,” Talia said.
